result: I keep thinking about the way the kitchen smelled when I was nine, the year my mother tried to grow basil on the windowsill and forgot to water it for three weeks. The leaves crinkled like old receipts, but the stems stayed green a little longer, as if they believed rescue was still possible. I used to whisper to them at dawn, pretending the plants could understand shame better than people. Now, twenty years later, I live in a fifth-floor walk-up with a single east window and three ceramic pots that drain too fast; every Saturday I overflow them on purpose, trying to flood away the memory of that first failure. Yesterday a storm came through, slapping the pane with warm rain, and I opened the sash so wide the thunder felt domestic. A wet piece of notebook paper flew in, pressed against my chest like a ransom note. The ink had bled, but I could still read one line: *don’t repeat the drought inside you.* I crumpled it, then smoothed it, then crumpled it again, finally tearing strips to mulch the soil around the new basil. It seemed fair: give the plants back the language I never knew how to use. This morning two tiny leaves have opened, spoon-shaped, still wearing yesterday’s storm. They don’t know my history; they only know the present damp. I touch them and feel the small pulse of water moving upward, reckless, against gravity, against every story I ever told myself about what can and cannot be revived.

It seemed fair: give the plants back the language I never knew how to use. This morning two tiny leaves have opened, spoon-shaped, still wearing yesterday’s storm.
